content.title: Limit of a Function
content.content:
Let $f: A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a limit point of $A$. We say 
$\displaystyle\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$, 
there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
0 < |x - c| < \delta \text{ and } x \in A \implies |f(x) - L| < \varepsilon.
\]

content.text:
Note the condition $0 < |x - c|$: we consider $x$ approaching $c$ but 
not equal to $c$. The function need not even be defined at $c$.

content.title: $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ Proof
content.solution:
Let $\varepsilon > 0$. We need $|3x - 1 - 5| < \varepsilon$, i.e., 
$|3x - 6| < \varepsilon$, i.e., $|x - 2| < \varepsilon/3$.

Choose $\delta = \varepsilon/3$. Then $0 < |x - 2| < \delta$ implies 
$|3x - 6| = 3|x - 2| < 3\delta = \varepsilon$.

content.title: A More Complex Limit
content.solution:
Let $\varepsilon > 0$. We have $|x^2 - 9| = |x + 3||x - 3|$.

We need to bound $|x + 3|$. If we require $|x - 3| < 1$, then $2 < x < 4$, 
so $|x + 3| < 7$.

Choose $\delta = \min\{1, \varepsilon/7\}$. Then if $|x - 3| < \delta$:
\[
|x^2 - 9| = |x + 3||x - 3| < 7 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{7} = \varepsilon. \qedhere
\]

content.title: Sequential Criterion for Functional Limits
content.content:
Let $c$ be a limit point of $\text{dom}(f)$. Then $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ 
if and only if for every sequence $(x_n)$ in $\text{dom}(f) \setminus \{c\}$ 
with $x_n \to c$, we have $f(x_n) \to L$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$(\Rightarrow)$ Suppose $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$. Let $(x_n)$ be a sequence 
with $x_n \to c$ and $x_n \neq c$. Given $\varepsilon > 0$, choose $\delta > 0$ 
as in the definition. Since $x_n \to c$, there exists $N$ such that 
$|x_n - c| < \delta$ for $n \geq N$. Then $|f(x_n) - L| < \varepsilon$.

$(\Leftarrow)$ Suppose the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ condition fails. Then 
for some $\varepsilon > 0$, for every $\delta = 1/n$, there exists $x_n$ 
with $0 < |x_n - c| < 1/n$ but $|f(x_n) - L| \geq \varepsilon$. Then 
$x_n \to c$ but $f(x_n) \not\to L$, contradicting the sequential criterion.

content.title: Divergence Criterion
content.content:
To show a limit does *not* exist, find two sequences approaching $c$ 
for which $f$ approaches different limits (or no limit at all).

content.title: Algebraic Limit Theorem for Functions
content.content:
Suppose $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ and $\lim_{x \to c} g(x) = M$. Then:

1. $\lim_{x \to c} (f(x) + g(x)) = L + M$

2. $\lim_{x \to c} kf(x) = kL$

3. $\lim_{x \to c} f(x)g(x) = LM$

4. $\lim_{x \to c} f(x)/g(x) = L/M$ provided $M \neq 0$
content.label: Theorem
